coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis of ionic liquid-based-carbon quantum dots as fluorescence probe for hemoglobin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav, Nisha
Mudgal, Deeksha
Mishra, Vivek
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a potential fluorescent probe in bio/analytical chemistry in the present decade. The optical characteristics of CQDs may be tuned by their functional groups, which can also be used to selectively produce stable bonds with target molecules. Along with them, ionic liquids (ILs) are now demonstrating their important relevance in the field of pharmaceuticals for the creation of potent therapeutics. In the article, we have discussed the use of high fluorescent ILs-decorated-CQDs (CQDs-IM@OTf) as a straightforward and quick-acting fluorescence probe for sensitive and precise hemoglobin (Hb) determination with minimum detectability of 6.7 nM. The proposed mechanism behind this involves static mode of quenching which leads to the formation of a ground state complex [CQDs-IM@OTf-Hb complex] between the Hb protein and the drug. Despite the fact that Hb can quench the fluorescence of CQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE) of the protein, which effects both the excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs, the addition of H2O2 improved the sensitivity of Hb detection. The present assay predicated on Hb interaction with H2O2, which produces reactive oxygen species such as hydroxyl (
O H
.
) and superoxide (
O 2
. −
) radicals under heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb. The subsequent reaction of hydroxyl radicals with CQDs, which acts as a strong oxidising agent, causes a high fluorescence quenching. The designed fluorescence probe was used to measure Hb in the concentration range of 3–90 nM with a precise detection limit of 0.33 nM. The quantification of hemoglobin (Hb) in diluted human blood samples is done using this observation.

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis of ionic liquid-based-carbon quantum dots as fluorescence probe for hemoglobin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Click Chemistry Research and Studies (AICCRS), Amity University, Noida, UP-201313, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Amity Institute of Click Chemistry Research and Studies (AICCRS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity Institute of Click Chemistry Research and Studies (AICCRS), Amity University, Noida, UP-201313, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a potential fluorescent probe in bio/analytical chemistry in the present decade. The optical characteristics of CQDs may be tuned by their functional groups, which can also be used to selectively produce stable bonds with target molecules. Along with them, ionic liquids (ILs) are now demonstrating their important relevance in the field of pharmaceuticals for the creation of potent therapeutics. In the article, we have discussed the use of high fluorescent ILs-decorated-CQDs (CQDs-IM@OTf) as a straightforward and quick-acting fluorescence probe for sensitive and precise hemoglobin (Hb) determination with minimum detectability of 6.7 nM. The proposed mechanism behind this involves static mode of quenching which leads to the formation of a ground state complex [CQDs-IM@OTf-Hb complex] between the Hb protein and the drug. Despite the fact that Hb can quench the fluorescence of CQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE) of the protein, which effects both the excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs, the addition of H2O2 improved the sensitivity of Hb detection. The present assay predicated on Hb interaction with H2O2, which produces reactive oxygen species such as hydroxyl (
O H
.
) and superoxide (
O 2
. −
) radicals under heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb. The subsequent reaction of hydroxyl radicals with CQDs, which acts as a strong oxidising agent, causes a high fluorescence quenching. The designed fluorescence probe was used to measure Hb in the concentration range of 3–90 nM with a precise detection limit of 0.33 nM. The quantification of hemoglobin (Hb) in diluted human blood samples is done using this observation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: A new family of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted significant attention due to its distinctive optical characteristics, including water solubility, biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and excellent optical and chemical stability. Much emphasis is placed on the analytical use of CQDs, similar to that of semiconductor quantum dots [1,2]. CQDs surface passivation and functionalization provide control over their physicochemical characteristics, demonstrating a strong bond with the target biomolecules [3–5]. Nevertheless, the produced CQDs fluorescence intensity is often more resistant to changes in the chemical or physical environment, such as ionic strength, pH, or despite the existence of several chemical species. Consequently, using these nanomaterials as fluorescence probes for the purpose of sensing chemical species that do not directly affect their fluorescence via a direct detection method might be a potential approach [6,7]. Additionally, ionic liquids (ILs) are attracting the attention of scientists all around the world as an alternative to inferior pharmaceuticals. Ionic liquids are organic salts with special qualities including strong thermal stability, low and abrupt melting temperatures, less vapor pressure, improved solubility, etc. They are also known as “designer solvents” because their physical and chemical characteristics may be adjusted to suit a variety of needs by varying the cation-anion combination [8–10]. The development of ILs in conjunction with CQDs improves the solubility of the drugs and, as a result, boosts their bioavailability, creating a huge research opportunity in the field of medicine and pharmaceuticals [11–13]. To understand pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interactions of drugs with human proteins is crucial for it to be competitive and effective. For a drug to be competitive and profitable, it is necessary to have insight about a drug's pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interactions with human proteins [14].

Human hemoglobin (Hb) is the most abundant protein in vertebrate erythrocytes and an iron-containing metalloprotein. It is the most significant component of RBCs (Red Blood Cells) and performs vital biochemical functions such as oxygen transfer and storage, electron transfer and metabolism, and so on. Hb's tetrameric structure, which consists of two α and two β subunits with a combined 572 amino acids, is held together by non-covalent forces such as electrostatic attraction, van der Waals contact, hydrogen bonds, and hydrophilic/hydrophobic interactions [15–17]. Many clinical disorders, such as leukaemia, anaemia, cardiac conditions, etc., are associated with the Hb level in blood. Hb levels that are considered normal for adults range from 13.5 to 17.0 g/dL for men, 12.0–15.0 g/dL for women, 11.0–12.0 g/dL for pregnant women, 14.0–24.0 g/dL for new-borns, and 11.0–16.0 g/dL for children. Worldwide, around 2 billion people affected with anaemia annually due to low level of Hb, including women and children. As a result, accurate Hb measurement in blood for medical diagnosis is critical for controlling such clinical disorders.

The fluorescence method is superior to other methods like high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical methods, colorimetry, and chemiluminescence for quantifying the level of Hb in blood because of its high selectivity and sensitivity, good reproducibility with accuracy, rapidity, and simplicity [6,18–21]. Nevertheless, the majority of fluorescence approaches exhibit very low selectivity and sensitivity, due to the use of transducers like metal complexes, dyes, and S-QDs. The mentioned transducer is then dominated by CQDs due to their distinct characteristics [22,23]. IL-decorated-CQDs then outperform the mentioned transducer due to their special characteristics.

In the present work, a new generation fluorescence probe i.e., CQDs-IM@OTf can be facilely developed by phase transfer by following one-pot synthesis of CQDs-IM from an unconventional precursor namely melamine and CMMIm-Br [3-(carboxymethyl)-1-methyl-1H-imidazole-3-ium bromide]. In this study, CQDs-IM@OTf were used as a fluorescent probe to provide a low-cost, straightforward, and sensitive approach for quantifying low levels of Hb. Despite the fact that Hb can reduce the fluorescence of CQDs based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the precision of Hb measurement is increased by H2O2. The quenching mechanisms of CQDs with Hb in the presence or absence of H2O2 were also discovered to be entirely different and were fully studied.

Melamine, 1-methyl-1H-imidazole, Bromoacetic acid, and sodium trifluoromethanesulfonate (NaOTf) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich and employed without further purification. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30% (v/v)), human serum albumin (HSA), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (S-Cys), riboflavin (RF), glutathione (GSH), cystine (SS-Cys), vitamin B12 (VB12), glucose (GOH), homocysteine (H-Cys), dopamine (DA), and salts NaCl, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2 and other chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. 30% H2O2 was diluted with Milli-Q water to create the H2O2 working solution. The CH3COONa/CH3COOH buffer solution (0.01 M, pH-5.0) was made by dilution in Milli-Q water of the desired amount and weight of CH3COOH and CH3COONa. The photo-physical investigations and all the tests were conducted using Milli-Q water. All characterization studies were conducted at room temperature.

Firstly, 1-methylimidazole (1.10 mmol) was mixed with 1 equivalent bromoacetic acid in 10 mL CH3CN and refluxed for 6h with continuous stirring and further isolated in the form of ionic liquid [CMMIm-Br. Further, Melamine (MA) and the synthesized [CMMIm-Br] were added to a round-bottom flask holding 25 mL of Milli-Q water, and the mixture was forcefully agitated to completely dissolve the solids. Generally, the [CMMIm-Br] quantity was altered from 1.05 g to 5.25 g while the melamine was kept at 1.0 g, resulting in a nMA/n[CMMIm-Br] ratio of 1:1 to 1:5. After thoroughly evaporating the Milli-Q water, the mixture was heated to 180 °C in an oil bath under inert atmosphere to produce a homogeneous yellow gel, which was subsequently pyrolyzed at 240 °C for varied lengths of time (1–2 h). The crude product was re-dispersed in 25 mL of Milli-Q water at room temperature, after which it was concentrated for 5 days using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da against Milli-Q water(Fig. 1 ). The resultant was an aqueous solution of CQDs-IM that was easily lyophilized and re-dispersed in Milli-Q water as needed.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 F electron microscope with a 200 kV acceleration voltage. The specimens were made by dropping the sample solution onto a copper grid that had been coated with carbon and then allowing it to dry at room temperature. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) with EDS was done from Amity University using Model Zeiss (EVO-18). The high-resolution powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern was produced using a CuKα radiation source and a PANalytical Empyrean X-ray diffractometer at an accelerating potential of 40 kV. The solid samples for the X-rays were finely powdered. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained on a Nicolet-5DX FTIR spectrophotometer, in the 400-4000 cm−1 range. Additionally, X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was collected using an Al Kα radiation-equipped PHI Versa Probe II. The optical and photoluminescence (PL) measurements were monitored through Duetta Absorbance and Fluorescence Spectrometer in the wavelength range 200–800 nm using a xenon lamp as an excitation source.

CQDs-IM were developed by pyrolyzing melamine (MA) and [CMMIm-Br], as schematically depicted in Fig. 1 serving Br− as the counterions in CQDs-IM. Briefly, the pre-mixed MA and [CMMIm-Br] were first heated at 180 °C in an open system until no water vapor could be seen. The mixture was then pyrolyzed at higher temperatures for varied lengths of time (1–2 h) until it transformed into a sticky yellow gel. After the reaction conditions have been systematically optimized (Table S1), CQDs-IM with the optimum PL-characteristics and maximum quantum yield were produced at a molar ratio of 1:3 between MA and [CMMIm-Br] after being pyrolyzed at 240 °C for 2 h. As-synthesized CQDs-IM were thoroughly characterized using a variety of characterization techniques, such as XRD, HRTEM, SEM, FTIR, XPS, and UV-PL analysis, to confirm the successful synthesis and reveal the intrinsic and morphological characteristics.

The FTIR spectra as shown in Fig. 2 a were recorded to investigate the successful synthesis of CQDs-IM, in the presence of various surface functionalities. FTIR spectra of CQDs-IM displays strong vibration band corresponds to CO of the carboxyl-group at 1621 cm−1 and the amide's spectral signatures centered at 1600 cm−1 (CO stretching of the amide bond) and 1536 cm−1 (in-plane N–H bending). Furthermore, it is clear from the spectra of CQDs-IM that virtually all the peaks from MA disappeared while those from [CMMIm-Br] (such as N–H stretching vibrations at 3117 and 3079 cm−1 and C–N stretching vibration at 1169 cm−1) persisted, indicating that during pyrolysis, MA completely decomposed while [CMMIm-Br] endured as the surface-modifiers of the CQDs-IM. Intriguingly, the reduction in vibrational band around ⁓3398 cm−1 in [CMMIm-Br] and disappearance of –NH2 stretching from MA, indicates that the free –NH2 group is no longer accessible and is instead linked via amide linkage [3,24]. Further, XPS spectroscopy was used to explore these functional groups. The survey scan in Fig. 2b reveals that the CQDs-IM produced are primarily composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, or C1s (75.03 at. %), N1s (9.91 at. %), and O1s (15.06 at. %), respectively. The deconvoluted C1s peak as shown in Fig. 2c confirms the presence of ILs-surface functionality, by displaying the peak of C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, -N-CO, and –OCO, which are located at 284.46 eV, 284.72 eV, 285.88 eV, and 287.38, respectively. Additionally, the deconvoluted N1s spectra in Fig. 2d where the primary peak (C–N–C) at 398.94 eV corresponds to the amide linkage in CQDs-IM, shows the presence of ILs-surface functionality. The deconvoluted O1s peak shown in Fig. 2e is finest matched by two Gaussian peaks at 530.85 eV and 532.14 eV. These peaks correspond to quinone-type CO and phenol C–O–H groups respectively [25].

The optical behaviour of synthesized CQDs-IM was also investigated using UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs-IM is depicted in Fig. 3 a, where a shoulder-shaped absorption peak at 340 nm is ascribed to n-ᴨ* transition of surface functional groups. The inherent CC and surface CO functional groups' ᴨ-ᴨ* and n-ᴨ* transitions are responsible for the rising absorbance between 500 nm and 300 nm. Moreover, the photoluminescence spectra of CQDs-IM reveals emission maxima at 450 nm for the excitation at 350 nm. When subjected to UV light using a 375 nm trans-illuminator, the CQDs-IM displays a reasonable amount of blue fluorescence in addition to having remarkable water-dispersibility and a light-yellow color under ambient light [26,27]. Additionally, excitation-dependent emission, an evident characteristic of many reported CQDs, was seen for CQDs-IM in aqueous medium. CQDs-IM emission curve measured at various wavelengths between 340 and 450 nm as displayed in Fig. 3b. As the excitation wavelength was raised, a red shift in the emission frequency and a quenching of fluorescence intensity were seen. Numerous theories have been put forth in an effort to figure out the method underpinning such a process. According to some findings in the literature, this is due to the small size of the particles as well as the quantum confinement effect. Others hypothesize that during carbonization, the sp3/sp2 hybridization ratio alters, resulting in the creation of hole pairs, which explains the luminescence behaviour of these CQDs [27,28].

A key component of employing CQDs-IM as a fluorescence probe in real-time applications is the stability of synthesized CQDs-IM. The CQDs-IM aqueous solution was stimulated under UV-light (365 nm) for 120 min at 30 min intervals to evaluate the photobleaching and stability of CQDs-IM. Only a 4% drop in fluorescence intensity was visible after 120 min (Fig. 3c). This demonstrates the excellent stability of the synthesized CQDs-IM. The inset images further demonstrate the photostability of CQDs-IM. The images taken after exposing CQDs-IM for 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min are virtually identical to the bright blue color seen by the naked eye (Fig. 3c).

Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-IM was determined using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis. The fluorescence lifetime decay of CQDs-IM as fitted by a tri-exponential function is shown in Fig. 3d. The average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-IM was determined to be 12.059 ± 0.484 ns at 350 nm excitation wavelength with an appropriate χ2 value (0.999).

Furthermore, the impact of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-IM has also been studied using buffer solution, over the pH range of 2.0–12.0. The isoelectric points and dissociation constants of the ampholyte groups such as carboxylic and phenolic hydroxyl groups connected to the surface of CQDs, exhibit high sensitivity to pH due to their fluctuating nature. It was intriguing to note that the fluorescence intensity only decreased by 20% in exceedingly pH-circumstances; pH below 3 or higher than 8 (Fig. 3e). Concluding, the steady performance of fluorescence was shown to be at its peak at neutral pH, as opposed to alkaline and acidic pH. Furthermore, the thermal stability of CQDs-IM was examined. We determined the heat decomposition temperature of the obtained CQDs-IM, by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The CQDs-IM tolerance at a certain temperature may be seen from the TG curve. As shown in Fig. 3f weight loss (about 2%) below 245 °C is the result of water evaporation in the ILs-functionalized CQDs. The process involves typical two-stage thermal degradation in which 1st-stage at 245–320 °C which corresponds to the removal oxygen-containing functional groups and hydroxyl groups on CQDs, as well as decarboxylation of carboxyl groups. The 2nd-stage is at 320–600 °C is mainly due to the cleavage of C–N bond, amide linkage and due to the thermal reduction of CQDs [29,30]. Owing to the multi-peak emission with good optical and thermal stability, the as-synthesized CQDs-IM behaves as a potent fluorescence probe for precise quantification of Hb.

The morphological and structural characteristics of as-synthesized CQDs-IM were first addressed using XRD analysis. The typical powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of CQDs-IM demonstrates a broad (002) diffraction peak at 24.9° (2θ), which equates to an interlayer spacing of 3.6 Å, showing its amorphous nature (Fig. 4 a). As shown by the related d-spacing of CQDs-IM, which is higher than that of pure graphite (
∼
3.3 Å), evident presence of turbostratic carbon structure (sp2 deformation). Additionally, predominantly graphitic-like structure reveals due to presence of one more peak centered around 43.2° (2θ) with an interlayer spacing of 2.09 Å [31,32]. Further, HR-TEM was used to analyse the morphology and microstructure of CQDs-IM in order to disclose their interior structure, as shown in Fig. 4b. Fig. 4b shows that as-synthesized CQDs-IM were spherical in nature with adequate monodispersion and a uniform size distribution (8.9 ± 1.34 nm, based on 100 counts), and no apparent agglomeration.

According to HRTEM pictures (Fig. 4b, inset), CQDs-IM particles have a noticeable lattice fringe, which indicates a high degree of graphitization. The (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphite carbon are represented by the lattice spacings of CQDs-IM consistent with XRD pattern, which are 0.36 and 0.21 nm, respectively [33]. Moreover, SEM imaging revealed that, during the carbonization process, the graphitic rod is pulled away, leaving behind carbon nanoparticles (CQDs-IM) that are roughly spherical in shape, consistent in distribution and size, and with a slight agglomeration effect. Meanwhile, it may be plausible that during the one-pot synthesis of CQDs-IM, due to ionic character and the clustering of electrons between the N-atoms of the imidazole rings (ILs), the spherical shape of the CQDs-IM unit is changed into a rod-shaped structure after the introduction of an ionic liquid onto the surface (Fig. 4c-e) [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase transfer of the CQDs-IM between water and ILs
ce:section.ce:para: The molecules with imidazolium moieties have tunable amphiphilicity, which can be achieved by merely designing the counter-anions. Up to this point, successful examples have been documented using functionalized gold nanoparticles [35], carbon nanotubes [36,37], and solid substrates like Si/SiO2 surfaces [38]. Herein, we introduce phase transfer between synthesized CQDs-IM [CQDs-IM+ and Br− as counter ions] and ethyl acetate (EA) in the presence of NaOTf. A biphasic system developed when EA (2 mL) was introduced to an aqueous-solution of CQDs-IM (2 mL, 0.5 mg mL−1) (Fig. 5 b). It was discovered that the CQDs progressively transformed into the EA phase after the addition of NaOTf, with a threshold NaOTf concentration of 0.2 mol L−1. Hereafter, CQDs-IM+ with OTf− as the counterion CQDs-IM@OTf displayed a λex-dependent PL emission after phase transfer with a comparatively greater fluorescence quantum yield (Ф, 14.28%) than that in water (12.36%) which may be related to the rise in purity brought on by extraction (Fig. S1) [39]. A thorough study showed that the polarity of the target organic solvent and the type of salts had to satisfy stringent standards for this phase transition [24]. Moreover, the reversibility of the phase transfer of CQDs-IM+ induced by hydrophilic salts is of considerable importance. It was discovered that, with the addition of NaBr, CQDs-IM+ could be returned to the water solution at a threshold NaBr concentration of 1.25 mol L−1. Other ions, such as NaCl, NaNO3, and KCl, were also added to accomplish this back-transfer from EA to water.

Hereafter, we shift to the phase transfer of CQDs-IM+ between water and ILs-OTf [CMMIm-OTf] (1a) containing imidazolium cation balanced with bulkier anion. When 1a (2 mL) was introduced to an aqueous-solution of CQDs-IM (2 mL, 0.5 mg mL−1), as a result of 1a′s greater mass than water, a system with two phases was formed, with 1a staying at the bottom (Fig. 5c). By following a straightforward handshake, the CQDs-IM+ that had previously remained at the top successfully transferred to the bottom phase (Fig. 5c). It should be noted that OTf, which initiates transition, is supplied in-situ by 1a, and anion exchange from Br− to OTf− happens concurrently with phase transfer. The removal of a third-party additive (such as NaOTf) during phase transfer simplifies the procedure and provides benefits in real - time applications. CQDs-IM+ in 1a exhibit λex-dependent emission (Fig. S2) with a substantially enhanced Ф of 18.37%, as in EA. However apart from the extraction effect, the remarkable enhancement in Ф could be attributed to CQDs-IM@OTf dispersibility in 1a, which is facilitated by the shared cationic moieties between solute CQDs-IM@OTf and solvent (1a).

Furthermore, we postulate that two reversible processes as shown in eqs. (1) and (2), respectively, are mainly in charge of controlling the phase transfer of CQDs-IM+ between water and 1a.
(1)
C Q D s − I M
k 1
⇄
k 2
C Q D s − I M − X
( X = O T f )
(2)
I L s − X
k 1
⇄
k 2
I L s − B r

The first equation is for changing the counterion of CQDs-IM+ and increasing the solubility of [CQDs-IM@X] (X = OTf) in the appropriate ionic liquid will enable phase transfer. According to the findings in the water/EA system, the more pronounced hydrophobicity of OTf is the cause of 1a′s superior phase transfer efficiency. The second step indicates the modification of the ILs' counterion, which occurs concurrently with the conversion of CQDs-IM to CQDs-IM@OTf. In this instance, we believe that the greater solubility of ILs-Br in water will contribute positively to phase transfer. Further, the effects of the initial concentration of CQDs-IM (cCQDs-IM) in the upper aqueous phase were studied (Fig. 5c & f). The photoluminescence of the upper aqueous phase is quite weak at cCQDs-IM < 0.10 mg mL−1, and the CQDs-IM@OTf absorption obeys the Lambert-Beer rule in 1a (Fig. S3), showing that the phase transfer of CQDs-IM+ from water to 1a occurs completely within this concentration range. After phase transfer, photoluminescence of the top aqueous phase can be noticed by both visual observation (Fig. 5C) and PL measurements at cCQDs-IM ≥ 0.10 mg mL−1 (Fig. 5f). The higher the cCQDs-IM concentration (<0.3 mg mL−1), the more prominent the upper aqueous phase photoluminescence. It was discovered that some CQDs-IM + can even remain at the phase borders for samples with cCQDs-IM >1.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. 5c). These findings show that partial phase transfer happens at cCQDs-IM ≥ 0.10 mg mL−1. The precise cCQDs-IM@OTf could be calculated (Table S2) using the absorbance coefficient of CQDs-IM@OTf in 1a found from the Lambert-Beer fitting (Fig. S3). The results of the PL readings showed that the PL intensity initially increases steadily with rising cCQDs-IM@OTf, reaches a maximum at cCQDs-IM@OTf = 0.35 mg mL−1, and then decreases with further increases in cCQDs-IM@OTf (Fig. S4) [40–42].

To ascertain whether it is possible to direct detect the concentration of Hb using CQDs-IM as a fluorescence probe, the variations in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-IM after the addition of Hb gradually in the range of 0–10000 nM were observed (Fig. 6 a and b). As can be seen, the presence of Hb causes a consistent decrease in fluorescence intensity as well as a steady redshift (of about 10 nm) in the emission spectra. However, an in-depth examination of Hb absorption spectra reveals that there is a significant amount of spectral overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs-IM (Fig. 3a). As anticipated, the spectral overlap of Hb absorptions with the CQDs excitation-spectrum causes a gradual decrease in the intensity of the overall emission spectral range. Additionally, the short-wavelength side of the emission spectra experience higher quenching due to the spectral overlap of Hb with the emission spectrum, resulting in a red-shifted emission spectrum. As a result, along with the fact that quenching excitation-wavelength-dependent, imply incorporation of inner filter effect (IFE) for the quenching of CQDs-IM by Hb. The IFE, which is a consequence of the excitation and/or emission light being absorbed by absorbers in the sensing device, plays a significant role in spectrofluorometry.

As a result, the CQDs-IM-based quantitative measurement of Hb has been developed. As depicted in Fig. 6a & b, the changes in the fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of CQDs-IM at peak values with increasing amount of Hb. I0 and I indicate the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Hb, respectively. The correlation of -log (I/I0) and Hb [nM] concentration exhibits a linear logarithmic behaviour with the equation -log (I/I0) = 0.7 × 10−3 [Hb] – (−2.3) × 10−2 with concentrations varying from 120 to 8000 nM and a correlation coefficient of 0.98. Likewise, based on the threefold standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity in the absence of haemoglobin, the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated to be 39.69 and 120.30 nM. Furthermore, it's interesting to note that at 200 nM of Hb concentration, CQDs-IM slightly alter the fluorescence intensity, but it quenched significantly (almost 90%) in presence of CQDs-IM@OTf (Fig. 6c & d). The proposed mechanism behind this involves static mode of quenching which leads to the formation of a ground state complex [CQDs-IM@OTf-Hb complex] between the Hb protein and the drug [43]. The equation for the quenching process, which has a correlation coefficient of 0.9980 and concentration ranges from 20 to 180 nM, is -log (I/I0) = 0.75 × 10−3 [Hb] – 3.6 × 10−2. The LOD and LOQ was estimated to be 6.7 and 20.30 nM, respectively. Additionally, the excellent % found and the small values of SD and %RSD for both CQDs-IM and CQDs-IM@OTf, as shown in Tables 1 and 2
demonstrate the precision of suggested methods.

It's intriguing to observe that although 100 nM of Hb or 1 mM of H2O2 separately slightly alter the fluorescence intensity, whereas their combination greatly reduces it (Fig. 6e & f). In addition, even at the quenched fluorescence, no obvious spectral shift in the emission bands was seen (Fig. 7 ). These findings clearly show that the quenching mechanism changes when H2O2 is present, and that the interaction between Hb and H2O2 most likely plays a significant part in the quenching of the fluorescence. To the best of our understanding, much consideration has been paid to the reaction of H2O2 with Hb as an iron-rich substance [44]. Sadrzadeh et al. (1984) discovered that Hb can function as a biological Fenton reagent. They hypothesised that the reaction's process is probably a Fenton-type reaction, in which ferrous heme iron breaks down H2O2 to generate metHb (HbFe3+), hydroxyl radicals (OH.), and hydroxyl anion (OH−) and ascribed the hydroxyl radicals to the active species created during the interaction between Hb and H2O2. Moreover, it has been observed that the interaction of Hb with a molar excess of H2O2 results in heme degradation and iron release. The authors determined that in the Fenton reaction, the released iron, not the heme iron, favours the creation of hydroxyl [45,46]. However, in accordance with the Haber-Weiss reaction, hydroxyl radicals can be produced by the interaction of superoxide and H2O2 triggered by transition metal ions, particularly iron [47]. Furthermore, CQDs have recently been described as fluorescence probes for detecting the reagents engaged in Fenton or Fenton-like reactions. Wei et al. (2014), for example, used gelatin-based CQDs to identify H2O2 and glucose in the presence of Fe2+, and ascribed the fluorescence quenching to the hydroxyl radicals generated during the reaction between H2O2 and Fe2+. According to these studies, H2O2 oxidises Fe2+ to Fe3+, and subsequent electron transfer between the excited-state of CQDs and surface-complexed Fe3+ ions result in intense fluorescence quenching [48].

It is well known that reactive oxygen radicals have a powerful oxidising tendency. Specifically, hydroxyl radicals with a redox potential of 2.74 V (vs standard hydrogen electrode, SHE) have an exceptionally powerful capacity to capture electrons [49]. Meanwhile, it has been observed that the surface states of CQDs have a significant impact on their fluorescence characteristics [50]. As a result, we can assume in this study that the hydroxyl radicals released in solution can oxidise the surface hydroxyl groups of CQDs, altering their surface properties and leading to fluorescence quenching. Fig. 7 depicts a suggested mechanism for fluorescence quenching of CQDs/H2O2 probe by Hb, which agrees with the documented results and our experimental data. In accordance with this mechanism, the interaction of Hb with H2O2 results in reactive oxygen species, such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, which are responsible for heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb. The subsequent interaction of hydroxyl radicals, which are potent oxidising agents, with CQDs causes the quenching of fluorescence.

Therefore, the CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 probe can be used as a novel fluorescence probe for the precise measurement of Hb. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 over time experiments with the presence of Hb were conducted (Fig. S5). The fluorescence intensity (I/I0) kept decreasing even after 40 min, while a stable state was achieved after 5 min of sonication. Therefore, all reaction mixture fluorescence intensities were measured after 5 min of sonication and 5 min of standing in order to have a fast-responding fluorescence probe. Further, the pH range 3.0–5.5 (CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer solution, 0.01 M) was examined to study the impact of pH on the probe response, and pH-5.0 showed the highest fluorescence quenching effectiveness (Fig. S6).

Hereafter, the precise measurement of Hb was carried out using CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 under optimal conditions. The probe's fluorescence spectra in the presence of varying Hb concentrations are displayed in Fig. 6e & f. The relationship between I0/I and Hb concentrations exhibits a linear logarithmic behaviour in dynamic range of 3–90 nM with a regression equation of -log (I/I0) = 1.68 × 10−2 [Hb] – 0.5 × 10−3 and a correlation value of 0.9993 (Fig. 6f, inset). The calculated LOD and LOQ values were 0.33 and 4.05 nM, respectively. Additionally, the excellent % found and low value of SD and high reproducibility with relative standard deviation (% RSD) serve as a proof of the suggested method's accuracy (Table 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hb in blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: We obtained blood samples from the VARDMAN Medicare Centre, Sec-5, Gurugram, health facility with its real Hb concentration (determined by immunoturbidimetric assay). We are following the Huan-Tsung Chang group's explanation of the sample preparation process. Briefly, 1 mL of blood samples was diluted 10 times, using Milli-Q water in sterilised vials. To lyse the erythrocytes, the containers were sonicated for 30 s. After lysis, the materials were centrifuged for 20 min. In order to conduct Hb determination experiments, the supernatants were gathered after the human serum albumin (and any potential interferences) settled as residue. We used a standard addition technique to identify Hb in blood samples while avoiding matrix interference. The standard Hb (100 nM–1000 nM) was inoculated in 3 mL of diluted supernatants samples (1000-fold reduction, 1 mL) and CQDs (1 mL). The mixture was then held at RT for 30 min Prior to measurement in a quartz cuvette, and the PL intensities were measured at excitation/emission wavelengths of 350/450 nm. The average of three replicates of Hb determination by a suggested technique concurred well with the immunoturbidimetric assay values (as tabulated in Table 4 ). Furthermore, we compared our analytical results obtained from capillary blood sample (Blood sample #4) with laboratory results. The results concluded that the present fluorometric-based method concurred well with immunoturbidimetric assay values with % recovery of 99.47 ± 3.329. The perfect agreement of the CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 probe's findings with the documented lab technique (Table 4) is very encouraging, showing the probe's outstanding performance and suitability for useful real-life application. Additionally, a single drop of blood is sufficient, so the patient's fingers can be used instead of a vein to gather the sample (Immunoturbidimetric assay).

Hereafter, to examine the selectivity of the suggested technique toward Hb, the interfering tests were conducted. Table 5 depicts the effects of some commonly occurring interfering species including cysteine (S-Cys), riboflavin (RF), glutathione (GSH), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), cystine (SS-Cys), vitamin B12 (VB12), glucose (GOH), homocysteine (H-Cys), dopamine (DA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl− and 100 nM Hb, at concentrations greater than their normal values. The result indicates that these interfering biomolecules do not show any serious interference in quantitative determination of Hb, as the %PL-intensity change ranged from +4.69 to −5.41%, demonstrating the selectivity of the probe and its dependability for pharmaceutical and biological applications. Other heme proteins including myoglobin and cytochrome C were also examined for their potential interference effects. Myoglobin and cytochrome C only have one heme group, therefore it stands to reason that they should have a smaller influence on PL-intensity than Hb, which has four heme groups in its structure and is present in the same quantity (as shown in Table 5). Although their concentration (1.6 μg/L and 27 μg/L, respectively) are significantly smaller than those of Hb (130–180 g/L, in adult men), their impact on the detection of Hb in blood may be disregarded [51]. The excitation/emission wavelength are 350/450 nm. To verify the viability, the as-synthesized fluorescent sensor was used to detect Hb in manufactured samples that contained interfering substances. Additionally, as shown in Table S3, the % recovery for these manufactured samples (n = 6) with low value of SD is 99.81 ± 1.72, along with high reproducibility with %RSD (1.72).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure the accuracy of Hb measurements, appropriate, high-quality procedures must be used in clinical labs and field settings [52–54]. The source of the blood sample, the cost of the analysis, and the repeatability of the results are a few things to think about while evaluating Hb assessment techniques and analysers [54–56]. The copper sulphate technique (CST) is one of the first qualitative procedures for evaluating Hb in clinical settings [57,58]. Later, quantitative techniques for measuring Hb concentrations, such as the cyanmethemoglobin (CM) method (1.0–8.0 g/L) [59,60], WHO color scale (0.5–10.0 g/L) [61], paper-/color-based analytical devices [62–64] (5.0–7.0 g/L) were developed. The reported techniques required the use of some risky chemicals, including O-toluidine and Drabkin's reagent, which consists of the extremely risky and harmful chemical likewise KCN along with controlled clinical laboratories environments. Further, a quantitative technique that was created to measure Hb in low-resource circumstances is the counting and size of particles using automated haematology analysers (AHAs) method in clinical labs only [65]. Despite the precise measurements of Hb in blood samples (artery, vein, and capillary), these clinical-based testing are not providing a filed-friendly method due to quality control calibration in presence of risky chemicals likewise Drabkin's reagent. However, our fluorometric based technique offers a clinical lab as well as field-friendly settings, in order to reduce the risk of any negative impact on Hb measurements [66,67]. The optimized CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 fluorescence probe, concurred well with required analytical conditions such as source of blood sample (artery, vein, and capillary), cost of the analysis and with excellent % recovery.

Additionally, the effectiveness of the CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 for the quantitative determination of Hb has been evaluated in comparison to its published methods for diagnosis. The published techniques [18,22,23,68–72] for the synthesis of quantum dots required high temperatures and long processes. In accordance to the data tabulated (Table 6 ), the Hb quantitative determination assay based on CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2 not only has the best sensitivity, but it also requires no costly materials or complex probe preparation and offers a straightforward and easy analysis. This advances the suggested technique for Hb determination and trace identification.

This study demonstrates, for the first time, one-pot development of CQDs-IM from melamine and CMMIm-Br to create a fluorescence probe, by modified their counter ion through phase transfer; CQDs-IM@OTf, for the precise measurement of Hb. The proposed probe demonstrated outstanding efficacy when exposed to H2O2 [CQDs-IM@OTf/H2O2], as a biological sensor with a LOD of 0.33 nM and adequate precision for the measurement of Hb at low concentrations. The designed probe was built in response to the reactive radicals that were produced when Hb and H2O2 reacted, quenching the CQDs' fluorescence. According to these findings, the developed nanoprobe should be used to identify small amounts of Hb in situations like hemolysis.
